concept: Kiss / Affection's Touch of the Lips
description: To kiss: to set the lips against someone or something in affection. The child's goodnight on a worn toy's nose, the parent's on a hurt knee, greeting, blessing, farewell. 'Phimela' is love made briefly physical; it asks nothing and closes nothing, the smallest complete gift the body gives.
sound_symbolism: 'phi' is the lips' own sound: the fricative 'ph' /ɸ/ is made exactly where a kiss is made, breath crossing lips barely parted. 'me.la' warms and releases it, the 'me' of 'melu' (friend) and 'mela' of gentleness, ending open: a kiss lands and lets go.
grammatical_notes: Transitive verb, the kissed as object: 'lopia tupiwa nophae phimela.' (The child kisses the rabbit's nose.) 'thema lopia to phimela.' (The guardian kissed the child.) The event noun, a kiss, comes by rule. Kin to 'palo' (touch), the wider sense it specializes, and 'lomare' (embrace), its larger sibling.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Metta with a location: goodwill condensed to one point of contact, offered and released without grasping.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A language for gentleness needs the act itself nameable: affection spoken as plainly as it is given.
